paragraphs.text: Student of the Jean Monnet Modules project “DERECHO”: ‘Defense of European Rights and Excellence for Consumers HHarmonisation’, No 101239437. Project financed by the European Union. Jean Monnet Actions in the field of Higher Education: Modules. Call for tenders ERASMUS-JMO-2025-HEI-TCH-RSCH. As regards the unlawfulness of dark patterns, I consider that there are issues of greater urgency and a more redacted solution, precisely because we take less account of the fact that we are not aware of a dark pattern. For example, social engineering cases are more complicated to resolve as they depend primarily on the human factor, both on the part of those who want to deceive the consumer and on the consumer himself. What I consider to be a main/fundamental idea of this digital fairness initiative is the distinction and categorisation of the different dark patterns, making it possible not only to identify more easily than one, but also to ‘guide’ in some way the solution to the problem. Among the different groups of dark patterns (obstruction, concealment, interface interference, forced action and social engineering), it would more rigidly adopt a more effective application of existing rules, or even new regulations, on obstruction dynamics (such as click-fatigue, taking into account the current average consumer’s imagery) and concealment (this could be said to be the most unlawful cases, because even being an informed and cautious consumer, hiding or playing with the information prevents the proper completion of any process in the digital world). Instead, with reference to cases of interface interference and social engineering, it would actively address consumer awareness and guidance, as these cases are largely dependent on the user’s own human factor and error.
